summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing an acute energy affordability crisis, with residential electric bills rising an average of 17.5% in a single year (2024–2025) — the steepest year-over-year increase in two decades — driven by a wave of IURC-approved rate hikes at Duke Energy Indiana, NIPSCO, AES Indiana, and CenterPoint Energy. The crisis has prompted an unprecedented political response: Governor Mike Braun replaced IURC commissioners, appointed a new consumer-focused Utility Consumer Counselor, signed landmark legislation (HEA 1002) introducing performance-based ratemaking and low-income protections, and the IURC launched a statewide Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in early 2026. Simultaneously, a rapidly expanding AI data center buildout across Indiana — particularly tied to NIPSCO's GenCo/Amazon deal — is raising new concerns from consumer advocates that future power demand will drive further rate increases for residential customers.

messaging_recommendations.message: Your utility bills have risen nearly 30% in a decade — you are not alone, and new protections under Indiana law (HEA 1002) mean you may be eligible for levelized billing, summer shutoff protection, and low-income assistance programs starting July 1, 2026. Know your rights and apply for the Energy Assistance Program each fall.

Performance-based ratemaking under HEA 1002 is a critical first step, but without a binding affordability standard — such as capping residential bill increases — and stronger IURC enforcement authority, Hoosier families remain exposed to the next round of utility rate requests; the data center growth wave makes action more urgent, not less.

The IURC's investigative inquiry has rebuilt public trust — now regulators must translate that momentum into durable, formal affordability standards, mandatory low-income assistance program quality benchmarks, and rigorous oversight of data center cost allocation to ensure residential ratepayers do not subsidize industrial growth.

Indiana's affordability crisis is not inevitable: it is the result of years of state policy choices that guaranteed utility profits regardless of performance. The same policy process that created this crisis can fix it — through transparent rate proceedings, consumer participation, and demanding utilities earn their returns by delivering reliable, affordable service.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana's general rate case requesting an approximately $491.5 million annual revenue increase (roughly 16.2%), filed April 2024. The IURC approved a reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, implemented in phases beginning March 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $295.7 million annual revenue increase approved; residential customer using 1,000 kWh/month saw approximately $19.16/month increase (~14-15% increase)

rate_cases.description: The Indiana Court of Appeals ruled in August 2025 that the IURC 'impermissibly applied' Indiana law retroactively when approving Duke Energy's coal ash compliance rate increase, reversing the IURC's decision and directing reconsideration or dismissal of the coal ash rate recovery component.

AES Indiana filed a Regulatory Rate Review on June 3, 2025, requesting a two-phase base rate increase of approximately 13.5% (~$21/month for a 1,000 kWh residential customer). A partial settlement reducing the increase by 53% to $102.3 million was filed October 2025; the OUCC and CAC oppose the settlement. The case is fully briefed with a final IURC order deadline of June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Originally requested ~13.5% ($21/month); settlement proposes ~$102.3 million increase; OUCC recommends rate decrease from current levels

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO's electric base rate case filed September 2024 requesting a large revenue increase. A settlement among NIPSCO, OUCC, and major industrial customers (US Steel, Walmart, etc.) reduced the increase to approximately $257 million. The IURC approved the settlement in June 2025, resulting in a 16.75% (~$23/month) average residential increase phased in July 2025 through Q1 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $257 million annual revenue increase; residential customers using 672 kWh/month saw ~$23/month (16.75%) increase phased in through Q1 2026

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy's electric base rate case filed December 2023 requesting an $118.8 million (16.02%) increase. The IURC approved an $80 million electric rate increase on February 3, 2025, phased in over two steps, increasing an average residential customer's bill by $11.18 in the first phase.
rate_cases.rate_change: $80 million annual revenue increase approved; residential customer (799 kWh/month) bill increase of approximately $11.18 in Phase 1, with Phase 2 bringing total increase to approximately $26/month

rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power announced IURC-approved rate reductions taking effect in the June 2026 billing cycle, following IURC approval. The average residential customer using 1,000 kWh/month will see a 3.6% (~$6) decrease in their monthly bill. I&M also announced a Customer Benefits Plan proposing further base rate reductions in a forthcoming rate case to be filed in summer 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: 3.6% decrease (~$6/month) for average residential customer using 1,000 kWh/month

articles.headline: How Indy's rising electricity bills stack up
articles.summary: Indianapolis residents are already paying about 27% more for electricity than five years ago and are facing another potential rate increase as the AES Indiana rate case heads toward a June 24, 2026 IURC order deadline. The article notes that energy costs and the impact of AI data centers have become a key political issue in Indiana. Earlier in 2026, the IURC launched an affordability investigation into the state's five largest electric utilities following a record number of consumer complaints.

articles.headline: Statehouse push for affordable power collides with data center growth
articles.summary: Indiana electric bills have risen nearly 30% over the last decade, with 2025 recording the steepest single-year jump. While the state government and IURC Chair Andy Zay are pushing for lower utility rates, Indiana is simultaneously welcoming a wave of power-hungry AI data centers, creating a tension between economic development goals and residential energy affordability. A Bloomington resident quoted in the article said she had to take out loans to keep the lights on while raising three children.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers told regulators their stacks of utility bills don't add up
articles.summary: Indianapolis residents aired complaints about billing spikes, metering problems, and data center costs at an IURC listening session held at Ivy Tech Community College, part of a statewide 10-stop listening tour. The session was part of the IURC's ongoing Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability launched in February 2026. IURC Chair Andy Zay said the commission will 'carefully study' the evidence collected and release a report with findings.

articles.headline: Gov. Braun signs HEA 1002 to lower Indiana energy costs
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002, landmark legislation that overhauls Indiana's utility regulation by introducing performance-based ratemaking and requiring utilities to meet measurable benchmarks for affordability and reliability. The law also requires utilities to offer low-income customer assistance programs by July 1, 2026, and automatically enrolls eligible low-income households in levelized billing plans. Unlike the previous system that effectively guaranteed utilities a return on equity, HEA 1002 ties returns to performance.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: The IURC held a daylong Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability with Indiana's five largest electric utilities — AES Indiana, CenterPoint, Duke Energy, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO — on March 24, 2026. Utility representatives attributed high bills primarily to customer usage patterns and infrastructure costs, while IURC Chair Andy Zay acknowledged the commission's role in approving rates and pledged formal follow-up actions. NIPSCO customers faced the highest bills and increases, at $234/month and a 27% hike in the most recent survey.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: The IURC announced it would conduct an unprecedented Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in response to growing customer outrage over high energy bills across Indiana. IURC Chair Andy Zay said 'that balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers' and called the hearing the beginning of a larger process to explore solutions. Citizens Action Coalition called the move 'unprecedented' but said it was 'long overdue.'

articles.headline: High Indiana NIPSCO utility bills a yearslong affordability crisis, advocates say
articles.summary: Consumer advocates describe Indiana's utility rate crisis — especially NIPSCO's — as the result of 15 years of state legislative and regulatory policies that gave utilities too much freedom to raise rates with little consumer protection. Kerwin Olson of Citizens Action Coalition stated that affordability remains the top issue Indiana residents face in 2026. The article notes that lawmakers and NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana were organizing protests over dramatically rising bills.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: Indiana residents will see new billing options and summer shutoff protections under HEA 1002, signed by Governor Braun in early 2026. The law creates a summer disconnection moratorium tied to extreme heat warnings (heat index of 95°F or above) and requires utilities to offer low-income assistance programs. AARP Indiana's Jason Tomsci called it a step in the right direction, noting that rate increases disproportionately impact older residents on fixed incomes.

articles.headline: Indiana Michigan Power to cut rates for utility customers starting in June
articles.summary: Indiana Michigan Power (I&M/AEP) announced that customers will see a 3.6% decrease (approximately $6/month) in their electric bills beginning with the June 2026 billing cycle, following IURC approval. The reduction comes as the IURC was holding listening sessions across Indiana as part of its broader energy affordability investigation. I&M's announcement was a notable contrast to the rate increases announced by most other Indiana utilities over the preceding two years.

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: An analysis of Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities showed bills rose 17.5% in a single year — an average of $28/month statewide — the steepest such increase in two decades. NIPSCO customers were hit hardest at $50/month (26.7%), followed by CenterPoint ($49/month, 24.9%), Duke ($26/month, 19.8%), AES ($17/month, 12%), and I&M ($7/month, 3.9%). AES Indiana was simultaneously seeking an additional $21/month increase through a pending rate case.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair
stakeholders.quote: "That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. … We're asking our five investor-owned utilities … to come to this room and have an open dialogue." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / IURC press conference

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair
stakeholders.quote: "I'm not dismissing, I'm not … deflecting responsibility. We have a role to play in this, absolutely … but that's a culture we're working on." (Direct quote, referring to the IURC's role in approving the high rates customers are now paying)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle — 'Big five' utilities defend rates in regulator questioning

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "It is a shame that it had to get to this point before they took action. It is long overdue. Ratepayers from Evansville to Gary have been organizing over outrageously high bills. Finally, someone is listening." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: Axios Indianapolis — Indiana's utility regulators call for investigation into affordability

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "Data centers are causing utility bills to go up for all customers" (Paraphrased from CAC presentation to Indianapolis City-County Council Environmental Sustainability Committee)
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition — Citizen Power April 2026 newsletter

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: OUCC Media Release (Cause No. 46258), reported in Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release / Daily Journal — Consumer advocates oppose proposed AES Indiana rate settlement

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is at a crisis point when it comes to our electric utility bills." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: WTHR — 'We can't take it anymore': Gov. Braun appoints official to fight utility rate hikes in Indiana

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "AI data centers are the number one threat to utility affordability, reliability, and environmental sustainability in Indiana. Elected officials should ensure that there are adequate rules in place to protect their constituents from the extremely … costs." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition press release — 27 Public Interest Organizations Call on Indiana Local Governments to Enact Data Center Moratorium

stakeholders.name: Megan Robertson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "Hoosier families are stretched thin, and they need relief now from monopoly utilities who've pushed policies for years that led to this crisis. This plan gives state leaders a clear, practical path to lower bills that will protect Hoosiers most in need and build a more affordable, reliable energy future for every community in our state." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: ICV/CAC joint press release — Ratepayer Relief Plan announcement

stakeholders.name: Jason Tomsci
stakeholders.title: Spokesperson
stakeholders.quote: "This is a pocketbook issue for older Hoosiers, because so many of them are on that limited income. Any increase to a bill, no matter how small it seems, has a real impact." (Direct quote, on HEA 1002)
stakeholders.source: WFYI Public Media — Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "At a time when customers are feeling pressure from rising costs, our responsibility is to ensure that growth works for them not against them. Affordability is one of Indiana's five core energy pillars, and it is central to our planning." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press release — AES Indiana and Google Partner to Deliver Over $770 Million in Customer Savings

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "Utility rates are too high, and my actions to stand up for ratepayers on appointments to the regulatory commission, bring new power to the grid, and overhaul how utilities are regulated to lower Hoosiers' bills is starting to pay off." (Direct quote)
stakeholders.source: Governor's office press release — Gov. Braun's Energy Affordability Strategy delivers early results for ratepayers
